title: Daily Routines
shortSummary: Describe your day, from waking up to sleeping, using everyday Hindi verbs.
culturalNote: A typical Indian day often begins early with chai (चाय), the spiced milk tea that fuels the country, and many households take a moment for prayer or a visit to a small home shrine. Lunch (दोपहर का खाना) and the evening meal (रात का खाना) are usually warm, freshly cooked, and shared, and a mid-afternoon cup of tea is a beloved ritual. In cities, long commutes shape the routine, while in villages life follows the sun more closely. Touching the feet of elders in the morning is a respectful way to start the day in traditional families. Saying you do something roz (रोज़, daily) or hamesha (हमेशा, always) is a natural way to describe habits, and the habitual present tense (with -ta/-ti hai) is the workhorse for routines.

keyPhrases.english: I wake up early in the morning (man)

keyPhrases.native: मैं दोपहर में खाना खाता हूँ
keyPhrases.transliteration: main dopahar mein khaana khaata hoon
keyPhrases.english: I eat lunch in the afternoon (man)
keyPhrases.pronunciation: main doh-puh-HAR mein KHAA-naa KHAA-taa hoon

keyPhrases.english: I take a walk in the evening (man)

grammar.explanation: To say something you do regularly, use the verb stem plus -ta (man) or -ti (woman) plus the form of hona. Main uthta hoon means 'I get up (regularly)'. This is the tense of routines and habits.

examples.native: मैं चाय पीता हूँ। (main chai peeta hoon.)
वह जल्दी सोती है। (vah jaldi soti hai.)

grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting the gender ending; a woman says peeti hoon, not peeta hoon.

grammar.explanation: Parts of the day take a postposition: raat ko (at night), subah (in the morning, often without a postposition), shaam ko (in the evening), dopahar mein (in the afternoon). These usually come near the start of the sentence.

examples.native: रात को मैं पढ़ता हूँ। (raat ko main padhta hoon.)
सुबह मैं दौड़ता हूँ। (subah main daudta hoon.)

grammar.commonMistake: Saying raat mein for 'at night'; the idiomatic phrase is raat ko.

dialogue.setup: Two friends, Rahul and Neha, talk about their daily routines over chai.

lines.speaker: Rahul
lines.native: नेहा, तुम सुबह कब उठती हो?
lines.transliteration: Neha, tum subah kab uthti ho?
lines.english: Neha, when do you wake up in the morning?

lines.speaker: Neha
lines.native: मैं छह बजे उठती हूँ और चाय पीती हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main chhah baje uthti hoon aur chai peeti hoon.
lines.english: I get up at six and drink tea.

lines.speaker: Neha
lines.native: मैं नहाती हूँ और काम पर जाती हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main nahaati hoon aur kaam par jaati hoon.
lines.english: I bathe and go to work.

lines.speaker: Rahul
lines.native: मैं भी रोज़ ऐसा करता हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main bhi roz aisa karta hoon.
lines.english: I do the same every day too.

lines.speaker: Neha
lines.native: शाम को मैं टहलती हूँ। और तुम?
lines.transliteration: shaam ko main tahalti hoon. aur tum?
lines.english: In the evening I take a walk. And you?

lines.speaker: Rahul
lines.native: मैं शाम को आराम करता हूँ और जल्दी सोता हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main shaam ko aaraam karta hoon aur jaldi sota hoon.
lines.english: In the evening I rest and sleep early.

quiz.question: How does a man say 'I drink tea' (habitually) in Hindi?
quiz.correctAnswer: मैं चाय पीता हूँ (main chai peeta hoon)
quiz.explanation: Peeta hoon is the masculine habitual present of 'to drink'.

quiz.question: Which is the correct way to say 'at night'?
quiz.explanation: Raat ko is the idiomatic phrase for 'at night'.

quiz.question: वह जल्दी _____ है। (She sleeps early)
quiz.explanation: Soti is the feminine habitual form of 'to sleep'.

quiz.question: What does 'मैं रोज़ ऐसा करता हूँ' mean?
quiz.explanation: Roz means 'daily' and karta hoon is the habitual present.

quiz.question: Arrange: हूँ / मैं / उठता / जल्दी (I wake up early)
quiz.correctAnswer: मैं जल्दी उठता हूँ (main jaldi uthta hoon)
quiz.explanation: Subject, adverb, then the verb phrase uthta hoon at the end.
